skill.name: research-hotspot-analysis
skill.description: Analyzes research hotspots and recommends literature for any disease or biomedical topic. Identifies current trends, clusters MESH terms into topics, and recommends high-impact papers.
skill.summary: Automated research hotspot discovery and literature recommendation from PubMed for biomedical topics.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: The static scanner flagged 75 potential issues but the vast majority (72) are false positives from markdown backtick formatting and pattern-based misdetections in documentation files. The confirmed finding is hardcoded NCBI API keys in scripts/analysis_ops.py, which is a credential exposure risk. Network requests to the legitimate NCBI PubMed API are expected for this skill's research purpose. The skill is legitimate with no malicious intent, but the hardcoded keys should be moved to environment variables.

high_findings.title: Hardcoded API Keys in Source Code
high_findings.description: Four hardcoded API keys for NCBI E-utilities are embedded directly in analysis_ops.py on line 282. API keys in source code can be extracted, abused beyond their intended scope, or rate-limited. These should be moved to environment variables or a configuration file outside the repository.

low_findings.title: External Network Requests to Third-Party API
low_findings.description: The script uses the Python requests library to call the NCBI PubMed E-utilities API. This is expected and necessary for the skill's stated purpose of searching medical literature, and the endpoint is a legitimate public research API.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded Credentials in Executable Script
dangerous_patterns.description: API keys for NCBI E-utilities are embedded as string literals in analysis_ops.py. This pattern exposes credentials in source code, making them accessible to anyone with access to the repository.

content.user_title: Analyze Research Hotspots in Medical Literature
content.value_statement: Researchers spend hours manually searching for trends in medical literature. This skill automates hotspot analysis by searching PubMed, extracting key topics from MESH terms, and recommending high-impact papers.
actual_capabilities: Searches PubMed for recent articles on any disease or research topic
Analyzes MESH terms to identify the most frequent keywords and themes
Clusters keywords into research hotspot topics using AI analysis
Matches specific PMIDs to each identified hotspot topic
Retrieves full article details for top-ranked papers
Generates a structured markdown report with hotspot analysis
limitations: Limited to PubMed-indexed literature only; does not cover preprints or non-indexed sources
Journal Impact Factor data is not available from the standard PubMed API
Maximum of 999 articles can be analyzed in a single session
Analysis quality depends on the accuracy of MESH term assignments in source articles

use_cases.title: Identify Emerging Research Trends
use_cases.description: A medical researcher wants to understand current hotspots in lung cancer research. The skill searches recent publications and clusters topics to reveal emerging areas.

use_cases.title: Literature Review for Grant Proposals
use_cases.description: A PhD student preparing a grant proposal needs a comprehensive overview of recent work in diabetes research. The skill analyzes top keywords and recommends key papers.

use_cases.title: Clinical Decision Support Background Research
use_cases.description: A clinician wants to understand the latest research trends in a specific therapeutic area. The skill provides a structured summary of current hotspots and high-impact publications.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Hotspot Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze research hotspots for {{disease}} using PubMed literature from the last 2 years. Run the analysis script and generate a report with the top keywords and topic clusters.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick analysis of a single disease or topic

prompt_templates.title: Detailed Literature Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform a comprehensive research hotspot analysis for {{disease}}. Search PubMed for up to 500 articles, identify the top 20 percent of MESH terms, cluster them into 5 to 8 hotspot topics, and recommend the top 3 papers per topic with full details.
prompt_templates.scenario: In-depth review for publications or grant writing

prompt_templates.title: Comparative Topic Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare research hotspots across {{disease_1}} and {{disease_2}}. Run separate hotspot analyses for each disease and highlight overlapping themes, unique topics, and differences in research focus areas.
prompt_templates.scenario: Comparing research trends between related diseases

prompt_templates.title: Custom Scope Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze research hotspots for {{disease}} with custom parameters. Set max results to {{max_results}}, focus on articles from the last {{years}} years, and generate a report with an introduction section, 5 hotspot topics with supporting PMIDs, and top paper recommendations per topic.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced users who need control over analysis parameters

output_examples.input: Analyze research hotspots for type 2 diabetes
output_examples.output: The analysis generated a research hotspot report with 6 identified topics. The top topic was Insulin Resistance and Metabolic Pathways with 47 associated articles. Key keywords included insulin resistance, beta-cell dysfunction, and inflammation. The most cited paper in this cluster had a PMC identifier available for full-text review.

output_examples.input: Find current trends in Alzheimer disease research
output_examples.output: The hotspot analysis identified 5 major research clusters from 200 recent publications. The strongest cluster was Amyloid-Beta and Tau Protein Mechanisms with 38 articles. Additional clusters included neuroinflammation, biomarkers, genetic risk factors, and therapeutic interventions. A markdown report was generated with recommendations for top papers in each cluster.

best_practices: Start with a broad disease or topic query to capture a wide range of relevant literature before narrowing results
Review the generated MESH term frequency list to verify the analysis captured the expected research areas
Use citation and PMC availability information to prioritize papers for full-text review
anti_patterns: Do not analyze overly narrow topics that return fewer than 20 articles, as the keyword frequency analysis needs sufficient data to identify meaningful clusters
Do not skip the manual review step; the hotspot clustering is AI-assisted and requires researcher validation for accuracy
Avoid using unspecific topics like medicine or health that return too many unrelated results and dilute the analysis

faq.question: What data source does this skill use?
faq.answer: This skill uses the NCBI PubMed API (E-utilities) to search for and retrieve article metadata including MESH terms.

faq.question: How many articles can be analyzed at once?
faq.answer: The analysis can process up to 999 articles in a single session. For larger analyses, run the skill multiple times with different search parameters.

faq.question: Does this skill support non-medical research topics?
faq.answer: The skill is designed for PubMed-indexed biomedical and life sciences literature. It may not work well for non-biomedical topics.

faq.question: What happens if no articles are found for my topic?
faq.answer: The skill returns an empty result set and displays the PubMed API error or zero-results response. You can try broadening your search terms.

faq.question: Does this skill require an API key to function?
faq.answer: The skill includes hardcoded API keys for NCBI E-utilities access. For production use, these should be configured as environment variables instead.

faq.question: Can the analysis results be exported?
faq.answer: The skill generates a structured markdown report containing the introduction, hotspot topics with associated PMIDs, and paper recommendations.
